titleStmt.title: Letter from the War Office to the Under Secretary, 31 December 1915
Letters 1916-1923

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from the War Office, London to the under-secretary, Sir Matthew Nathan (1862-1939), regarding the issuing of permits for civilians from Government Departments travelling to the British Zone at the front. The letter suggests that a regulation, similar to those in force in the War Office (‘Permits for Civilians Proceeding to France on Duty' which is attached) should be established in each Department. Sir Matthew Nathan was the under-secretary for Ireland from 1914 to 1916. In 1916 Nathan offered his resignation following the outbreak of the Easter Rising.

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers, CSO RP/1916/5618/26

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
PERMITS FOR CIVILIANS PROCEEDING TO FRANCE ON DUTY Any civilian proceeding on duty to the zone of operations
in France is required to obtain a passport, and to apply
at the Military Permit Office, 19 Bedford Square, with a view
to its being vise just in the same way as a civilian travelling
on private affairs. He should take the passport to the Military
Pormit Office with a certificate from the head of the branch by
which the order to proceed to France is issued to the effect
that he is travelling on duty and that the necessary steps have
been taken either through the Adjutant-General, Expeditionary
Force, or the Inspector-General of Communications, as the case
may be, to ensure that a pass permitting him to proceed to his
destination will be found awaiting him at the office of the
Assistant Provost-Marshal at the port of disembarkation. The
head of the branch will be responsible for informing the
Adjutant-General, Expeditionary Force, or the Inspector-General
of Communications, either by letter or in urgent cases by telegram that, if
there is no objection, it is proposed to send .............. to
.......... on .......... for the purpose of .......... and that
it is requested that a pass may be issued and await him at the
Assistant-Provost-Marshal's office at ............ Office Memorandum No.812
7th September, 1915.
War Office, London, S.W. 31st December, 1915 Sir, I am commanded by the Army Council to acquaint you that
the Commander-in-Chief, British Army in the Field has represent
ed that officials of different Government Departments occasion
ally arrive within the British Zone of the Armies without any
previous communication having been addressed to him on the
subject of their intended visit. He requests that to avoid
difficulty, every Department should adopt regulations similar
to those in force at the War Office, a copy of which is attached. The Council would be glad to be informed of the name and
designation of the official of your Department who may be
appointed to grant the certificate to which reference is made
in the Regulations and to move the military authorities in
France to issue the necessary pass. These will be communicated
to the Adjutant General, Expeditionary Force and the Inspector
General of Communications for their information, and will be
recorded at the Military Permit Office, Bedford Square. I have the honour to be,
Sir,
Your obedient servant, The Under Secretary Irish Office.
